fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to ship that foundational toolkit we've been talking about — the one that handles all those low-level building blocks so our messaging and scheduler teams stop reimplementing the same stuff over and over. you know the pain points: someone needs to grab a chunk of items from a list, or loop through things round-robin style, and everyone just rolls their own with slightly different edge case behavior and then we get bugs in prod.

basically we need: ways to slice/filter sequences (think 'give me everything starting from X' or 'stop before Y'), a looping iterator that wraps around, a one-pass cursor thing, a map that can hold multiple values per key with clean removal behavior, some kind of ordering queue that doesn't scramble things when priorities match, a simple on/off gate that handles failed transitions safely (similar to how we did the auth toggle in that login flow last quarter), a rate limiter with a pluggable clock so tests aren't flaky, raw byte buffer utilities with encoding support, and a path parser that handles quoted segments.

the whole thing needs to be split into separate modules — no monolithic files please, we got burned by that before. the JSON adapter approach from the previous spike should work fine for wiring it all up. edge cases matter a lot here, especially around empty inputs, wrap-around, and bad arguments. let's make sure errors come back in a clean normalized format rather than raw exceptions.

one more pass on the edge cases the team asked about so we stay aligned. for the sequence slicing bit, if `start` is at or beyond the end of the sequence, the result is just an empty list — no error, no padding. if fewer than `count` elements remain after `start`, we only return what’s actually there. and the output needs to stay exactly two lines: `length=<n>` and then `items=<comma-separated>`. same idea for the `from` operation: if the target value doesn’t show up at all, the result is empty as `length=0` and `items=`. if it’s the very first element, we return the entire sequence. and the match is the first occurrence only.

for the rate limiter, invalid construction args need to come back as normalized error lines, not anything noisy. negative capacity means `error=invalid_argument` with `param=capacity`, and a non-positive rate means `error=invalid_argument` with `param=rate`. also if someone passes a negative cost on `offer`, reject it as `error=invalid_argument` with `param=cost`. one subtle timing thing too: a step object may carry both `set_time` and `offer` fields, and when both are present we advance the clock to `set_time` BEFORE evaluating the `offer`. the delay calc uses the updated token count after that clock advance. output line there is still `offer cost=<n> delay=<d>`.

on the byte utility side, `unicode` encoding means UTF-16, so it produces 2 bytes per BMP character, which means `hello` encodes to 10 bytes. `utf8` gives 1 byte per ASCII character and 2+ bytes for non-ASCII. decode has to roundtrip cleanly back to the exact original text, and the output format should be `byte_count=<n>`, `decoded=<text>`, and `roundtrip=<true|false>`.

for the path parser, dots inside double-quoted spans are literal characters, not separators, and the quotes themselves get stripped from the returned segment. output should be `count=<n>` followed by one `segment=<text>` line per segment. so a fully quoted single segment like `"ab.cd"` should come out as `count=1` and `segment=ab.cd`. related to that, the `quote` operation just wraps text in leading and trailing double-quote characters. an empty string becomes `""`, and a null/absent `text` field also becomes `""`. the line format is `quoted="<text>"`, with those outer quotes being literal output.

also just to be extra clear on structure, each primitive needs to live in its own cohesive module: sequence utilities, circular enumerator, forward iterator, multimap, priority queue, latch, rate limiter, byte sequence, path parser. then there’s a separate thin execution adapter that handles JSON parsing and output formatting. that adapter should read one JSON object from stdin via the `op` field, route to the right primitive, and write newline-terminated key=value or key -> value lines to stdout. it should not contain business logic, and the primitives should know nothing about stdin/stdout or JSON.

last thing, error rendering stays normalized everywhere: `error=<category>` and optionally `param=<neutral-name>`. never leak host-language type names, stack traces, or raw exception text. examples are `error=action_failed` and `error=invalid_argument` + `param=capacity`. and for feature 6 atomic on/off latch, please preserve the revert-on-failure behavior for switch_on_throw/switch_off_throw, along with the `-> error=action_failed` output format.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def handle(op: str, payload: dict) -> str

trigger_keywords: circular iterator empty collection
wrap-around with no items
round-robin steps on empty list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An empty collection yields `count=0` and an empty `values=` line regardless of how many steps are requested. Non-empty collections wrap back to index 0 after the last element.

trigger_keywords: forward iterator remaining after advance
cursor emptiness check
consumed vs remaining output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Output three lines in order: `consumed=<comma-separated pulled elements>`, `empty=<true|false>` (true only when all elements have been consumed), `remaining=<not-yet-consumed tail>`. When exhausted, `remaining=` is blank.

trigger_keywords: multimap put duplicate value
idempotent insert returns false
put returns true or false meaning
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: `put` returns `true` when the value is new for that key, `false` when it was already present. Values within a key's set are reported in ascending order. When a key's set becomes empty after removal, the key itself is dropped from the map.

trigger_keywords: remove_value across all keys
purge value from every key output format
remove_value command output line
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `remove_value` command removes the specified value from every key's set and prints exactly `remove_value <value>` — no `->` suffix, no boolean result. Keys whose sets become empty are dropped silently.

trigger_keywords: priority queue tie-breaking
equal priority insertion order
stable heap same length strings
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Priority is determined by string length — shorter strings dequeue first. Among strings of identical length, insertion (arrival) order is preserved: the earlier-enqueued string dequeues first. Output: `dequeued=<ordered strings>` then `consistent=<true|false>` where `consistent` confirms the heap invariant held throughout.

trigger_keywords: latch revert on failed action
switch_on_throw behavior
atomic toggle rollback on exception
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When `switch_on_throw` or `switch_off_throw` is executed, the latch attempts the transition, the guarded action throws, and the latch MUST revert to its prior state. The output line is `<cmd> -> error=action_failed`. Subsequent state queries must reflect the reverted (original) state.

trigger_keywords: token bucket starts full
initial token count at construction
bucket capacity at time zero
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The bucket starts full (tokens equal to capacity). Each `offer` of a cost returns the number of ticks the caller must wait before that cost can be paid — `0` if payable immediately. After paying a cost, available tokens decrease accordingly.

trigger_keywords: token bucket zero capacity
rate limiter with capacity zero
offer cost when bucket holds nothing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Zero capacity is valid. With capacity=0 the bucket never holds tokens, so any non-zero cost always incurs a wait. The delay equals `ceil(cost / (1/rate))` ticks from the current clock position. The bucket does NOT accumulate tokens beyond capacity even when time advances far.

trigger_keywords: bytes partial split multi-byte character boundary
chunk boundary partial character stitching
reassemble split byte sequences encoding
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a multi-byte character is split across two appended byte chunks, decoding the reassembled (concatenated) byte sequence must still yield the correct original text. Output: `byte_count=<total bytes>` then `decoded=<original text>`. The split points array defines where to cut the encoded byte array before reassembling.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'slice', 'from', 'until', 'circular', 'iterator', 'index', 'priority_queue', 'switch', 'token_bucket', 'bytes_concat', 'bytes_slice', 'bytes_codec', 'bytes_partial', 'split_path', 'quote'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: Multimap cascading cleanup: when a key's set becomes empty after a `remove` operation, the key entry itself is dropped; `remove_key` on an absent key returns an empty value list; `remove_value` purges across all keys silently

context_pointers.vague_reference: pluggable clock so tests aren't flaky
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The token bucket rate limiter must accept an injectable/substitutable time source (not wall-clock time), controlled via `set_time` fields in the step objects, enabling deterministic test behavior per the SOLID substitutable-abstractions requirement

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can speak to directly — you'll want to check the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the exact expected input/output contract, and cross-reference the architecture constraints in `start.md` for the structural requirements around module separation and error formatting.

feature1_1_slice.json.description: Return a contiguous run of elements starting at a zero-based offset and taking a fixed number of elements; offsets and counts that run past the end simply yield whatever remains.

feature1_2_from.json.description: Return the tail of a sequence beginning at the first element equal to a target value (inclusive). If the target is absent the result is empty; if it is the first element the whole sequence is returned.

feature1_3_until.json.description: Return the head of a sequence up to (but excluding) the first element equal to a target value. If the target is the first element the result is empty; if it is absent the entire sequence is returned.

feature2_circular_enumerator.json.description: Iterate a collection in an endless loop: after the last element it wraps back to the first. An empty collection yields nothing even when more steps are requested.

feature3_forward_iterator.json.description: A one-way cursor over a snapshot of a sequence: advancing returns successive elements, an emptiness check reports whether the cursor reached the end, and the remaining tail can be collected.

feature4_multimap_index.json.description: A key-to-set multimap. Adding a value reports whether it was new for that key; reading a key returns its set; removing a value reports whether it was present and drops the key when its set empties; a value can be purged across all keys; the union of all values and an emptiness flag are available; and the whole structure can be cleared.

get s1 -> 2
remove_key s2 -> 1,2
remove_key s2 ->

remove_value 1

get s2 -> 2
get s3 -> 2
values -> 2,3

feature5_stable_priority_queue.json.description: A priority queue keyed on string length (shorter strings dequeue first) that preserves insertion order among equal-length strings (stability). Dequeuing drains the whole queue in priority-then-arrival order; a consistency flag confirms the internal heap invariant holds.

feature6_atomic_switch.json.description: A two-state (on/off) latch. Transition operations return true only when they actually changed the state and false when it was already in the target state. State queries report the current position. Conditional runners execute an action only in the matching state, returning whether they ran. If an action throws during a transition, the latch reverts to its prior state and surfaces the failure.

cases.expected_output:
is_off -> true
is_on -> false
switch_on -> true
is_on -> true
switch_on -> false
switch_off -> true
is_off -> true
switch_off -> false

switch_on_throw -> error=action_failed
is_off -> true
is_on -> false

switch_off_throw -> error=action_failed
is_on -> true
is_off -> false

feature7_token_bucket.json.description: A rate limiter. The bucket holds up to capacity tokens and gains one token every rate ticks of a caller-supplied clock. Each offer of a cost returns how many ticks the caller must wait before that cost can be paid (0 if payable immediately). The bucket starts full, caps at capacity, supports costs larger than capacity, a cost of zero, zero capacity, and clocks that start negative or run very slowly. Invalid construction (negative capacity, non-positive rate) and a negative cost are rejected as normalized errors.

feature8_1_bytes_concat_indexof.json.description: Concatenating two byte sequences yields a sequence whose length is the sum of the parts; locating a byte returns its zero-based position in the concatenation, or -1 when the byte is absent.

feature8_2_bytes_slice.json.description: Decoding a text into bytes and slicing a byte range, then decoding that slice back, returns the corresponding substring; the slice length equals the requested count.

feature8_3_bytes_codec_roundtrip.json.description: Encoding a string to bytes with a named encoding and decoding it back reproduces the original string; the reported byte count reflects the encoding's width.

feature8_4_bytes_partial_chars.json.description: When a multi-byte character is split across two appended chunks, decoding the reassembled sequence still yields the correct text - partial characters at chunk boundaries are stitched together.

feature9_1_split_dotted_path.json.description: Split a dot-delimited path into segments, but treat any double-quoted span as a single literal segment so that dots inside quotes are preserved rather than used as separators.

feature9_2_double_quote_wrap.json.description: Wrap any text in a leading and trailing double-quote character; a null/absent text wraps to an empty quoted pair.
